edges.description: Failed proximal tubular cystine reabsorption raises urinary cystine to the poorly soluble range that drives cystine nephrolithiasis.
Defective proximal tubular reabsorption of filtered cystine produces the biochemical cystinuria phenotype.
Failed proximal tubular dibasic amino-acid reabsorption increases urinary arginine excretion.
Failed proximal tubular dibasic amino-acid reabsorption increases urinary lysine excretion.
Failed proximal tubular dibasic amino-acid reabsorption increases urinary ornithine excretion.
The same transporter defect disrupts renal handling of cystine and dibasic amino acids.

Additional urinary and systemic stone-risk chemistry abnormalities are reported in cystinuria, but the cached evidence does not establish whether they are direct consequences of the cystine transporter defect or co-occurring modifiers.
Poor cystine solubility permits crystallization in urine, initiating crystal nucleation and growth.
Supersaturated urinary cystine crystallizes, producing cystine crystalluria.
Prenatal echogenic colon is an occasional cystinuria-associated finding; the cached evidence does not resolve the intermediate mechanism.
Low urinary citrate is represented as a stone-risk chemistry modifier.
Elevated urinary calcium is represented as an occasional stone-risk chemistry modifier.
Elevated urinary uric acid is represented as an occasional stone-risk chemistry modifier.
Elevated blood uric acid is represented as a reported systemic stone-risk chemistry feature of cystinuria.
Crystal growth and aggregation convert cystine crystallization into clinically significant recurrent stones.
Recurrent stones damage the kidney through recurrent renal injury, obstructive uropathy, and repeated urologic intervention.
edges.target: Chronic Kidney Injury from Recurrent Stones

edges.description: Recurrent cystine stone formation manifests clinically as nephrolithiasis.
Recurrent cystine stones and urinary tract involvement can be accompanied by recurrent urinary tract infections as a recognized complication of cystinuria.
Hematuria is reported as a very frequent phenotype in cystinuria stone disease, but the cached evidence does not specify the local injury mechanism.
Flank pain is reported as a frequent phenotype in cystinuria stone disease, but the cached evidence does not specify the intermediate pain pathway.
Nausea and vomiting are reported in cystinuria stone disease, but the cached evidence does not specify the intermediate pathway.
Abnormal urinary odor is reported in cystinuria, but the cached evidence does not define its intermediate mechanism.
Chronic kidney disease and cumulative renal injury reduce renal function, captured clinically as renal insufficiency.
edges.source: Chronic Kidney Injury from Recurrent Stones

edges.description: Chronic kidney disease and cumulative renal injury can contribute to high blood pressure in cystinuria cohorts.
edges.source: Chronic Kidney Injury from Recurrent Stones
High fluid intake and dietary sodium/protein moderation
Calculi removal for symptomatic or large cystine stones

nodes.description: Cystinuria disrupts renal handling of cystine and dibasic amino acids.

evidence.snippet: HP:0004337 | Abnormality of amino acid metabolism | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Abnormal urinary odor is listed among frequent urinary findings.

evidence.snippet: HP:0012088 | Abnormal urinary odor | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Alpha-lipoic acid is an investigational approach supported by mouse-model evidence and a clinical trial evaluating daily supplementation for cystine stone recurrence.

evidence.reference_title: alpha-Lipoic acid treatment prevents cystine urolithiasis in a mouse model of cystinuria.
evidence.snippet: α-lipoic acid inhibits cystine stone formation in the Slc3a1-/- mouse model of cystinuria by increasing the solubility of urinary cystine.

evidence.reference_title: The Effect of Lipoic Acid Natural Supplement on Cystine Stone Formation
evidence.snippet: This study evaluates how daily alpha lipoic acid supplementation affects cystine kidney stone recurrence.

nodes.description: Urinary arginine excretion is increased as part of the dibasic amino-acid transport defect.

evidence.reference_title: Reference values of urinary excretion of cystine and dibasic aminoacids: classification of patients with cystinuria in the Valencian Community, Spain.
evidence.snippet: Means for cystine, lysine, arginine ornithine and their sum in homozygotes and heterozygotes non-type I were significantly (p < 0.001) in excess of the respective reference ranges.

nodes.description: Endoscopic or surgical stone removal, including ureteroscopic management and lithotripsy when appropriate, treats symptomatic or large cystine stones but does not correct the underlying transport defect.
nodes.id: Calculi removal for symptomatic or large cystine stones

evidence.reference_title: Ureteroscopic management in cystinuric patients: long-term results from a tertiary care referral center.
evidence.snippet: The management protocol consisted of (i) treating symptomatic or > 7 mm stones, (ii) multi-staged URS for voluminous stones

nodes.description: Recurrent stones, obstruction, and repeated urologic procedures can damage kidney tissue over time, contributing to chronic kidney disease and hypertension.
nodes.id: Chronic Kidney Injury from Recurrent Stones

evidence.reference_title: Cystinuria: genetic aspects, mouse models, and a new approach to therapy.
evidence.snippet: CKD in patients with cystinuria may result from recurrent stones, repeated urologic interventions, or obstructive uropathy

nodes.description: Cystine crystals nucleate, grow, and aggregate in urine, converting supersaturation and crystalluria into clinically significant stones.

evidence.reference_title: Cystinuria: genetic aspects, mouse models, and a new approach to therapy.
evidence.snippet: In addition to crystal nucleation and growth, crystal aggregation is required for stone formation

nodes.description: Hexagonal cystine crystals can be detected in urine when cystine exceeds its solubility threshold.

evidence.snippet: HP:0033067 | Cystine crystalluria | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Tiopronin or D-penicillamine can form more soluble mixed disulfides and are used for severe or recurrent stone disease not controlled by conservative measures and alkalinization.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation and Medical Management of Patients with Cystine Nephrolithiasis: A Consensus Statement.
evidence.snippet: using cystine-binding thiol drugs

evidence.reference_title: Cystinuria: genetic aspects, mouse models, and a new approach to therapy.
evidence.snippet: These sulfhydryl drugs convert cystine to the more soluble mixed disulfides of cysteine-penicillamine or cysteine-tiopronin, respectively.

nodes.description: Increased urinary cystine is the biochemical hallmark of cystinuria.

evidence.snippet: HP:0003131 | Cystinuria | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Cystine stones commonly cause flank pain or renal colic.

nodes.description: Blood in the urine occurs with cystine stones and stone passage.

nodes.description: High urine volume plus sodium and animal-protein moderation are first-line conservative measures to reduce cystine concentration and stone recurrence.

evidence.snippet: conservative measures: fluid intake and dietary modification

nodes.description: Recurrent cystine kidney stones are the defining clinical manifestation.

nodes.description: SLC3A1 or SLC7A9 dysfunction impairs b(0,+)-mediated proximal tubular reabsorption of filtered cystine and dibasic amino acids.

evidence.snippet: causes a failure in the reabsorption of filtered cystine and dibasic amino acids in the proximal tubule

evidence.reference_title: Cystinuria: genetic aspects, mouse models, and a new approach to therapy.
evidence.snippet: The COLA transporter (b0,+) is a heterodimer consisting of the rBAT (encoded by SLC3A1, located on chromosome 2p16.3) and b0,+AT (encoded by SLC7A9, located on chromosome 19q13.1) subunits joined by a disulfide bridge.

nodes.description: Cystine stones form repeatedly in the kidney and urinary tract, causing renal colic, hematuria, obstruction, and recurrent intervention.

evidence.snippet: The clinical symptoms of this disease are caused by the loss of poorly soluble cystine, which precipitates to form stones.

nodes.description: Recurrent urinary tract infections are a recognized complication of cystinuria and its recurrent urinary tract stone burden.

evidence.snippet: Complications of cystinuria include recurrent urinary tract infections, chronic kidney disease (CKD), and hypertension.

nodes.description: Recurrent stones, obstruction, procedures, and chronic injury can reduce renal function.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000083 | Renal insufficiency | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.snippet: SLC3A1 (chromosome 2p21) encodes the heavy subunit rBAT of a renal b(0,+) transporter

evidence.reference_title: Twenty-four novel mutations identified in a cohort of 85 patients by direct sequencing of the SLC3A1 and SLC7A9 cystinuria genes.

evidence.snippet: Genetic analysis of both cystinuria genes may be necessary due to the synergistic effects of mutations in two genes.
SLC7A9 | HGNC:11067 | cystinuria | MONDO:0009067 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: High urinary cystine concentration exceeds cystine solubility, especially when urine volume is low or pH is not sufficiently alkaline, producing cystine crystalluria.

evidence.reference_title: A Summary of Current Guidelines and Future Directions for Medical Management and Monitoring of Patients with Cystinuria.
evidence.snippet: increased urine levels of the poorly soluble amino acid result in recurrent cystine nephrolithiasis

evidence.reference_title: Cystinuria: genetic aspects, mouse models, and a new approach to therapy.
evidence.snippet: Of the four amino acids, cystine is the least soluble and it can crystallize in the urinary tract, leading to cystine stone formation.

nodes.description: Alkali therapy, commonly potassium citrate, raises urine pH to improve cystine solubility when conservative measures are insufficient.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation and Medical Management of Patients with Cystine Nephrolithiasis: A Consensus Statement.
evidence.snippet: first alkalinizing the urine and then using cystine-binding thiol drugs
